docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:111:## 2. Inventory: the UFO side
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:113:### 2.1 UFO-A: endurants and type structure
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:115:UFO’s primary distinction is between:
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:120:A unique `Kind` supplies the identity principle governing which changes an endurant can undergo while remaining the same individual. UFO distinguishes types and individuals explicitly and formalizes instantiation and specialization in first-order modal logic. **[LIT-BACKED]** ([Guizzardi et al., “UFO: Unified Foundational Ontology”](https://nemo.inf.ufes.br/wp-content/uploads/ufo_unified_foundational_ontology_2021.pdf))
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:122:Important UFO-A categories include:
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:137:`Kind` in UFO is rigid and identity-supplying. `Role` is anti-rigid and relationally conditioned. `Phase` is anti-rigid and intrinsically conditioned. A `Relator` is a reified, existentially dependent relational complex mediating multiple individuals. These are not merely labels for ordinary-language “kind,” “role,” “state” or “relationship.” **[LIT-BACKED]**
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:139:### 2.2 UFO-B: events and participation
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:141:UFO-B covers:
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:149:- atomic and complex events and, in fuller UFO work, processes
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:151:An endurant participates in a perdurant through a manifestation of a disposition inhering in that endurant. Thus participation is more formally committed than merely appearing as a grammatical argument of `HAPPEN`. **[LIT-BACKED]** ([UFO overview](https://www.inf.ufes.br/~gguizzardi/UFO-Story.pdf))
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:153:### 2.3 UFO-C: intentional and social entities
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:155:UFO-C builds on UFO-A and UFO-B and covers:
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:169:The bridge pattern is not UFO-specific:
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:171:| UFO | DOLCE analogue | BFO analogue |
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:182:DOLCE is linguistically and cognitively oriented and therefore a natural secondary comparison target. BFO is a useful interoperability target for the programme’s OBO assets. UFO is the best primary target here because it supplies the richest formal account of `Kind`, `Role`, `Phase`, relators and intentional/social entities. **[PROVISIONAL]** ([DOLCE overview](https://www.loa.istc.cnr.it/index.php/dolce/); [BFO 2020](https://bfo-ontology.github.io/bfo-2020.html))
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:193:- A UFO category classifies entities or types and is constrained by a formal theory.
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:195:- Some UFO notions are meta-properties of types, not ordinary first-order concepts.
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:201:- **Ø — no direct category mapping:** the prime belongs to logic, discourse or lexical semantics rather than a UFO base category.
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:203:### 3.2 Correspondence table 1: primes to UFO
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:205:| Kernel primes | Closest UFO target | Status | Analysis |
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:207:| `I`, `YOU` | Particular agent/person in a discourse role | P | Indexical reference identifies speaker/addressee. UFO supplies no universal category “I”; the referent may be modeled as a human agent playing a conversational role. |
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:208:| `SOMEONE` | Human agent/person | P | `SOMEONE` supplies personhood in NSM, while UFO `Agent` includes physical and social agents and is defined through intentional moments and actions. It is narrower in one respect and broader in another. |
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:209:| `PEOPLE` | Collection or plurality of human agents | P | May denote several persons, a collective, or people generically. UFO distinguishes plural quantification, collectives and social agents; NSM `PEOPLE` does not decide among them. |
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:210:| `SOMETHING~THING` | Entity, individual, object or endurant | P | It is a general non-person substantive, not an exact synonym for UFO `Object` or `Endurant`. It does not by itself distinguish enduring things, events, qualities, relators or abstract entities. |
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:211:| `BODY` | Physical object / biological endurant | A/P | A strong anchor for a physical endurant, but UFO still requires identity and constitution choices—for example, person versus body, organism versus matter. |
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:212:| `KIND` | Type/class; sometimes UFO `Kind` | P | Major false-friend risk. NSM means “things of one kind”; UFO `Kind` is specifically a rigid sortal supplying identity. Most NSM `KIND` uses justify only `Type`, not `ufo:Kind`. |
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:213:| `PART` | General parthood | A/P | Strong anchor for a part-whole relation. It does not entail UFO’s reflexivity, transitivity, supplementation, category restrictions, essential parthood, membership, componenthood or functional dependence. |
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:217:| `ONE`, `TWO` | Cardinality | A | Good anchors for exact small cardinalities in an executable logic, subject to scope and unique-name assumptions. They are logical resources, not UFO categories. |
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:219:| `MUCH~MANY`, `LITTLE~FEW` | Vague quantity/cardinality regions | P | Require contextual thresholds or quality regions. UFO does not provide those thresholds automatically. |
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:220:| `GOOD`, `BAD` | Evaluative quality/value or judgment | P | May be modeled through qualities, value regions, goals or normative descriptions. They are context-sensitive evaluators, not generic UFO `Quality` instances by themselves. |
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:222:| `THINK` | Mental event/state or intentional moment | P | UFO-C can model a mental moment or its manifestation. The prime does not settle whether the referent is an enduring mode, a state or an event occurrence. |
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:223:| `KNOW` | Belief/knowledge state with factive content | P | UFO-C has belief and propositional content, but belief is not knowledge and does not automatically encode factivity or justification. |
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:224:| `WANT`, `DON'T-WANT` | Desire/aversion | A/P | Strong anchors for UFO-C desire-like intentional moments. Reification, inherence and propositional content require the UFO layer. |
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:227:| `SAY` | Communicative action/event | A/P | Usually aligns with an intentional action. UFO-C additionally models speaker intention, content, participants and possible social effects. |
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:229:| `TRUE` | Truth/satisfaction relation over propositional content | P/Ø | Better mapped to formal semantics than to a UFO base category. UFO-C propositions and situations can support satisfaction, but truth is not reducible to an entity class. |
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:230:| `DO` | Action or agent participation | A/P | Strong action anchor when the actor is intentional. NSM `DO` is broader than UFO-C intentional `Action`, so intentionality cannot be assumed from syntax alone. |
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:231:| `HAPPEN` | Event/perdurant | A | The strongest prime-to-UFO anchor. Even so, `HAPPEN(p)` is a predication; an ontology must separately reify or type the event and identify its participants and temporal parts. |
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:232:| `MOVE` | Movement/change event | A/P | A natural subtype of event/process. UFO classification still needs the mover, trajectory and event identity. |
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:234:| `THERE-IS` | Existence | A/P | Aligns with existential assertion. UFO’s modal and time-indexed existence predicates add machinery absent from the prime. |
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:236:| `IS-MINE` | Possession/ownership relation or social relator | P | Ownership commonly needs agents, norms, claims and a social relator. The prime supplies first-person possession, not UFO’s full ownership structure. |
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:237:| `LIVE` | Biological state/process | P | A domain type under event/enduring biological organization, not a UFO foundational category. |
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:245:| `NOT` | Logical negation | A | Maps to formal apparatus, not a UFO category. Open-world negation, explicit negative facts and failure-to-prove must remain distinct. |
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:246:| `MAYBE` | Epistemic/modal possibility | P | Does not choose UFO’s metaphysical possible-world modality, epistemic uncertainty or probabilistic confidence. |
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:247:| `CAN` | Modal possibility, capacity or disposition | P | Critical ambiguity. It may motivate a UFO `Disposition/Capability`, but can also express permission or situational possibility. |
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:248:| `BECAUSE` | Causation, explanation or reason | P | UFO-B includes causation, but natural-language explanatory dependence is broader than event causation. |
docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:252:| `LIKE~AS~WAY` | Similarity, manner or analogy | P | UFO has no single base category corresponding to all these readings. Similarity requires a comparison basis. |

item.text:
## Verdict

[well-established] Every listed UFO-A moment can be represented as RDF 1.2 data, including statements about statements. [well-established] RDF 1.2 triple terms denote propositions without asserting them, while `rdf:reifies` introduces a separately describable reifier; N3 additionally quotes whole graphs/formulae and supports rules over them. [RDF 1.2 Concepts](https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf12-concepts/#section-triple-terms), [N3 specification](https://w3c.github.io/N3/spec/).

[well-established] The remaining limitation is not reification but inference strength: OWL-RL plus safe Horn-N3 can execute structural moment rules, whereas unrestricted UFO dependence and disposition semantics quantify modally over possible circumstances/worlds. [plausible] Those modal conditions can be operationalized decidably over an explicit finite world/event carrier; complete unrestricted first-order-modal reasoning is not decidable in general.

Assume these prefixes:

```turtle
@prefix ufo: <urn:ufo:> .
@prefix ex:  <urn:ex:> .
@prefix rdf: <http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#> .
```

## Per-construct result

| Construct | Concrete encoding / required rule | Verdict |
|---|---|---|
| Quality | `ex:q1 a ufo:Quality; ufo:inheresIn ex:apple; ufo:hasQuale ex:red37.` [well-established] The quality is an individual node, distinct from its bearer and value. | **Yes** [well-established] |
| Quality structure / quale | `ex:red37 a ufo:Quale; ufo:inStructure ex:CIELAB; ufo:coordinate [ ufo:dimension ex:aStar; rdf:value 37 ].` [plausible] Regions, dimensions, ordering and coordinates are ordinary RDF resources. [plausible] Known projections/comparisons can use ground N3 numeric built-ins; an arbitrary metric needs a deterministic builtin or authored relation. | **Yes; computation may need a builtin** [plausible] |
| Mode | `ex:belief7 a ufo:Mode; ufo:inheresIn ex:alice.` A quality of the mode is simply `ex:intensity7 a ufo:Quality; ufo:inheresIn ex:belief7.` [well-established] | **Yes** [well-established] |
| Disposition | `ex:fragility1 a ufo:Disposition; ufo:inheresIn ex:glass; ufo:triggerCondition ex:tc1; ufo:manifestsIn ex:breaking1.` [well-established] Activation and manifestation are reified events: `ex:impact1 a ufo:Activation; ufo:activates ex:fragility1.` and `ex:breaking1 a ufo:Manifestation; ufo:manifestationOf ex:fragility1.` [plausible] A trigger proposition can be `ex:tc1 rdf:reifies <<( ex:glass ex:struckBy ex:hammer )>>.` | **Partly in RL; yes with modal/finite-world rules** [plausible] |
| Relator | `ex:employment1 a ufo:Relator; ufo:mediates ex:alice, ex:acme; ufo:hasPart ex:aliceQuaEmployee, ex:acmeQuaEmployer; rdf:reifies <<( ex:alice ex:employedBy ex:acme )>>.` [well-established] Here the relator is both a relationship truthmaker and an RDF reifier associated with the material-relation proposition; RDF does not force those roles to be identical, so the UFO typing supplies that commitment. | **Yes structurally; modal dependence needs rules** [well-established] |
| Qua-individual | `ex:aliceQuaEmployee a ufo:QuaIndividual, ufo:Mode; ufo:inheresIn ex:alice; ufo:externallyDependsOn ex:acme; ufo:properPartOf ex:employment1.` [plausible] The reciprocal qua-individual is encoded analogously. | **Yes** [plausible] |
| Externally dependent mode | `ex:commitment1 a ufo:Mode; ufo:inheresIn ex:alice; ufo:externallyDependsOn ex:bob.` [well-established] `owl:differentFrom` or a validation constraint should establish that the external dependent is not the bearer; RDF/OWL has no unique-name assumption. | **Yes structurally; partly modally** [well-established] |
| Existential dependence | `ex:commitment1 ufo:existentiallyDependsOn ex:alice, ex:bob.` [well-established] Its modal force is supplied by the explicit-world rule below, not by the bare RDF property. | **Partly in bare RDF; yes in the finite-world rule profile** [well-established] |
| Inherence | `ex:q1 ufo:inheresIn ex:apple.` [well-established] Inherence may be declared a subproperty of specific existential dependence, while its fixed-bearer/non-migration constraints are expressed by rules or validation. | **Yes structurally; partly modally** [well-established] |

## Minimal executable N3 extension

```n3
# Inherence entails existential dependence.
{ ?m ufo:inheresIn ?b. }
=>
{ ?m ufo:existentiallyDependsOn ?b. }.

# A dependent present in a represented world entails its bearer/dependent there.
{
  ?x ufo:existentiallyDependsOn ?y;
     ufo:existsIn ?w.
}
=>
{ ?y ufo:existsIn ?w. }.

# A relator depends on every entity it mediates.
{
  ?r a ufo:Relator;
     ufo:mediates ?x;
     ufo:existsIn ?w.
}
=>
{ ?x ufo:existsIn ?w. }.

# Trigger satisfaction activates a disposition.
{
  ?d a ufo:Disposition;
     ufo:triggerCondition ?condition.
  ?condition ufo:obtainsIn ?w.
}
=>
{ ?d ufo:activatedIn ?w. }.

# Normalize manifestation direction.
{ ?d ufo:manifestsIn ?event. }
=>
{ ?event ufo:manifestationOf ?d. }.
```

[well-established] These are range-restricted Horn rules and terminate over a finite RDF carrier when built-ins do not invent unbounded new terms. [well-established] OWL 2 RL itself has polynomial-time standard reasoning problems and is designed for rule-engine implementation. [OWL 2 Profiles](https://www.w3.org/TR/owl2-profiles/#OWL_2_RL).

[plausible] Counterexample validation—“`x` exists in world `w`, but its required bearer does not”—needs a closed, complete world graph plus scoped negation-as-failure, SHACL, or stratified Datalog. [well-established] Under RDF’s open-world semantics, absence of `y ufo:existsIn w` is not evidence that `y` does not exist there.

[speculative] If the semantics must create previously unknown manifestation events or possible worlds, use a guarded or weakly-acyclic existential-rule/chase fragment. [well-established] Arbitrary existential rules or unrestricted N3 rules do not preserve the termination/decidability guarantee of OWL-RL plus safe Datalog.

## Family verdict

| Family | Verdict | What is needed |
|---|---|---|
| Quality and Mode | **Yes** [well-established] | Reified nodes, `inheresIn`, quale/structure resources; numeric built-ins only when projections must be computed. [plausible] |
| Disposition | **Partly in OWL-RL; yes in an explicit operational profile** [plausible] | Reified disposition, activation and manifestation nodes; quoted trigger formula; finite-world modal rules for counterfactual force. [plausible] |
| Relator and qua-individuals | **Yes structurally; partly for full modal axioms** [well-established] | Reifier/truthmaker node, mediation, qua-parts and dependence rules. [plausible] |
| Inherence and external/existential dependence | **Yes as statements; partly as unrestricted UFO semantics** [well-established] | Safe Horn propagation over explicit worlds is decidable; unrestricted possible-world quantification requires a richer modal/FOL engine. [well-established] |
| All listed moments together | **Yes representationally; partly in OWL-RL+N3 execution** [well-established] | No statement-reification gap remains. [well-established] Full reference-UFO modality remains outside the existing tractable profile, not outside RDF/N3 syntax. [plausible] |

## Corrections to the local analysis

[well-established] The absolute claim in [expressivity-analysis.md](/home/ec2-user/css/kernel/kernel-of-truth/docs/next/arch/expressivity-analysis.md:148) that RDF/N3 has no encoding for operators over propositions is too strong: RDF 1.2 represents single propositions as triple terms, and N3 represents compound quoted formulae. [plausible] The defensible replacement is: “quoted content is statable, but its domain-specific modal/intensional consequences require explicit rules or another entailment regime.”

[well-established] The “missing” reification, inherence, relator and disposition structures listed in [nsm-ufo-bridge.md](/home/ec2-user/css/kernel/kernel-of-truth/docs/next/arch/nsm-ufo-bridge.md:437) are missing from the NSM profile, not inexpressible in RDF. [plausible] Its narrower distinction between executable OWL-RL and full reference-UFO modality remains valid.
